content.text: A booking and travel agent is an agentic AI system that manages the full lifecycle of trip planning and booking: searching for flights and accommodation, comparing prices across providers, selecting options that match user preferences, placing reservations, assembling itineraries, and handling changes and cancellations — all driven by a natural-language instruction such as "book me a return flight to Tokyo for two weeks in September, under $1,200, with a window seat."
What is it?

Unlike a static travel comparison site, a booking agent reasons across multiple constraints simultaneously — dates, budget, maximum layover duration, hotel proximity, cabin class, airline loyalty programmes, and corporate travel policies — and proposes an optimised bundle rather than presenting a raw list for the user to evaluate manually. The agent operates in an agentic loop: it queries multiple booking APIs in parallel, applies preference filters, ranks options, presents a recommendation for user approval, and then places the hold or reservation once confirmed.

The agent's defining properties are constraint-aware search and confirmation-gated execution. Search is autonomous — the agent fans out across providers and applies preferences without user input on each step. Booking, however, requires an explicit confirmation gate: no payment-bearing action is taken without the user reviewing and approving the proposed option. This boundary separates read-only exploration (where autonomy adds value) from irreversible financial commitments (where human oversight is mandatory).
Why it matters

Booking a multi-leg international trip with specific constraints — preferred carrier, maximum layover, hotel tier, proximity to specific venues — typically requires 60 to 90 minutes of manual search across multiple sites, comparison of dozens of options, and careful application of loyalty programme rules. For frequent travellers and teams that manage corporate travel at volume, this overhead is significant and structurally repetitive: the same constraints apply to every trip, yet every trip requires the same manual process.

Corporate travel represents an additional layer of complexity. Companies enforce travel policies — preferred carriers, maximum fare classes, expense caps per night by city, approval chains for trips above a threshold cost — that must be checked for every booking. Without automation, this creates friction between the traveller (who must navigate the policy) and the approver (who must verify compliance). A booking agent that has the policy encoded can surface only compliant options, reducing both the traveller's cognitive load and the approval overhead.
Benefits
Constraint-aware search
— filters and ranks options against budget, timing, cabin class, airline alliance, hotel star rating, and proximity to specific addresses without the user visiting each provider separately.
Cross-provider aggregation
— fans out to Amadeus, Skyscanner, Booking.com, Expedia, and activity platforms in parallel, collapsing a multi-site comparison into a single ranked recommendation.
Preference persistence
— loyalty programme numbers, seat preferences, meal requirements, and travel policy rules are applied automatically on every search, without the user re-entering them.
Proactive change management

— monitors booked itineraries for schedule changes, airline-initiated cancellations, or significant price drops on unbooked legs, and surfaces rebooking options without waiting for the user to notice.
End-to-end orchestration
— handles multi-leg trips, sequences hotel check-in and check-out dates to match flight arrival and departure, and outputs a single itinerary document with all confirmation numbers.
Policy compliance enforcement
— for corporate deployments, filters search results to compliant options before presenting them, eliminating the need for travellers to manually cross-check against the travel policy.
Trade-offs & limitations
Payment security boundary

— the agent must either operate through a pre-authorised payment instrument or hand off at the checkout step. Direct card processing by the agent introduces PCI DSS scope and significant security risk; most production deployments use a hold-then-confirm flow.
API coverage gaps

— not all accommodation providers expose real-time availability APIs. Boutique hotels, short-term rentals outside major platforms, and some regional carriers require web scraping or human fallback, creating inconsistent coverage across provider types.
Dynamic pricing staleness

— flight and hotel prices change on the order of seconds. A price quoted during the search phase may not be available by the time the booking is confirmed. The agent must handle hold expiry, re-pricing, and user notification when the price changes between proposal and confirmation.
Cancellation and change complexity

— cancellation and change policies vary across carriers, booking classes, and hotel types. Automatically evaluating whether it is better to pay a change fee versus rebook requires policy data that is not always available via API.
Corporate policy engine complexity

— enterprise travel policies can be elaborate: per-city nightly caps, preferred vendors by country, approval chains by trip cost, exceptions for specific departments. Encoding these correctly requires a structured policy model, not simple preference matching.
Confirmation reliability

— confirmation number and itinerary extraction from email relies on the provider's email template. Format changes on the provider side can silently break extraction, causing the agent to operate on stale confirmation data.
How it works
A booking and travel agent operates across four sequential phases for each booking request:
Phase 1: Request parsing and constraint extraction

The agent parses the natural-language request to extract structured constraints: origin and destination airports or cities, travel dates (outbound and return), number of passengers, budget ceiling, cabin class, any explicit carrier preferences, and accommodation requirements (location, tier, check-in/check-out dates). It also loads the user's persistent preference profile (loyalty numbers, seat preference, meal requirement, policy rules) and merges it with the per-request constraints to form the full search specification.
Phase 2: Search and aggregation

The agent fans out search queries in parallel to flight search APIs (Amadeus GDS, Skyscanner), hotel search APIs (Booking.com, Expedia), and car rental or ground transport APIs if required. For multi-leg itineraries, it generates the sequence of flight segments from the destination list and departure dates, then searches each segment independently. Results are normalised to a common schema — price, duration, stops, airline, loyalty points earned — that enables cross-provider comparison.
Phase 3: Ranking and recommendation

The ranking function scores each option against the preference model. A scoring function weighs price against budget headroom, preference fit (does the airline match the preferred alliance? is a window seat available?), convenience (total travel time, number of stops, layover duration), and loyalty value (points earned per dollar of spend). The top-ranked options across flights and hotels are assembled into a candidate itinerary, and the total cost is calculated against the trip budget.
Phase 4: Confirmation gate and booking

Before any booking action is taken, the agent surfaces the proposed itinerary to the user: ranked options, total cost, trade-offs noted (e.g., the cheapest option has a 4-hour layover; the preferred airline is $130 more but direct). The user approves one option. The agent then places a 24-hour hold on the flight (available via most GDS APIs) and a provisional hold on the hotel, giving the user time to review before payment is committed. The actual payment step is handed off to the booking provider's checkout flow — the agent does not handle card data directly.
Architecture overview

attrs.alt: Booking and travel agent: travel requests flow into an agent core with request parser, preference model, availability aggregator, price optimizer, itinerary builder, and confirmation gate, producing search and booking actions across flight, hotel, car, and activity APIs

content.text: The availability aggregator is the key performance-sensitive component. It fans out to N search APIs in parallel and aggregates results within a latency budget. The preference model is loaded once per session and applied as a filter at the aggregator output before results reach the price optimizer. The confirmation gate is a hard checkpoint: no write action (hold, book, cancel) bypasses it. The itinerary builder assembles the confirmed components into a structured document, syncs confirmation numbers to the user's calendar, and monitors the itinerary post-booking for schedule changes.
When to use it
A user books 4 or more trips per year and spends significant time evaluating flight and hotel options against the same recurring constraints.
A company manages travel for employees at volume and needs consistent enforcement of a travel policy across all bookings without manual policy review per trip.
A trip involves multiple legs, multiple destinations, or multiple travellers whose itineraries must be coordinated simultaneously.
The traveller participates in multiple airline and hotel loyalty programmes whose rules need to be applied to maximise points earned on each booking.
The use case requires proactive monitoring of booked itineraries for cancellations, schedule changes, or significant price movements on unbooked segments.

A booking agent adds less value for occasional travellers with simple point-to-point trips and no strong preferences, or for bookings in categories that lack reliable API coverage (e.g., niche accommodation types, small regional carriers). In these cases, manual search is faster than building and maintaining the integration overhead.
How to implement
Step 1: Define the preference and policy model

Model the user's (or company's) travel preferences as a structured JSON document: preferred airlines and airline alliances, cabin class rules, maximum layover duration, hotel tier by destination category, loyalty programme identifiers, meal preferences, and budget defaults. For corporate deployments, add the travel policy layer: per-city nightly caps, preferred vendor lists, expense approval thresholds, and department-specific exceptions. This model is loaded at session start and injected into the agent system prompt.
Step 2: Integrate search APIs

Start with the two most impactful integrations: a flight search API (Amadeus or Skyscanner) and a hotel search API (Booking.com or Expedia). Normalise the response schemas to a common internal format that abstracts over provider-specific field names. Add provider integrations incrementally rather than attempting to integrate every possible source before shipping. For providers without public APIs, evaluate whether a web-based booking link fallback is sufficient for the initial implementation.
Step 3: Implement the ranking function

Define a scoring function that weighs each option against the preference model. A practical scoring approach: start with a pass/fail filter for hard constraints (budget ceiling, max stops), then score soft constraints on a 0-10 scale (airline preference, seat availability, loyalty value, total travel time). Combine scores with weights that reflect the user's priority ordering (budget-conscious users weight price heavily; time-sensitive users weight duration). Expose the weights in the preference model so they can be updated without code changes.
Step 4: Build the confirmation and payment gate

Categorise all actions by reversibility and financial impact. Read actions (search, price check, seat map query) run autonomously. Hold actions (24-hour flight hold, provisional hotel hold) require a single user confirmation presenting the proposed itinerary and total cost. Payment-completing actions (finalise booking) are handed off to the provider's checkout URL rather than executed by the agent directly. Cancellations and rebookings always surface the cost impact (change fee, fare difference) before execution.
Step 5: Handle itinerary monitoring and change management

After booking, register the confirmed PNR numbers and reservation IDs with a monitoring service that polls for schedule changes, cancellations, and significant price movements. When a change is detected, the agent should: classify the change (minor schedule adjustment vs. cancellation vs. route change), retrieve alternative options within the original constraints, and surface the situation and proposed rebooking to the user rather than acting autonomously on confirmed bookings.
Examples
Flight search agent with preference filtering and hold

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# User travel preferences stored as a persistent profile
USER_TRAVEL_PREFS = {
    "cabin_class": "economy",
    "preferred_airlines": ["JAL", "ANA", "Singapore Airlines"],
    "max_layovers": 1,
    "max_layover_duration_hours": 3,
    "seat_preference": "window",
    "loyalty_numbers": {
        "JAL": "JL1234567",
        "ANA": "ANA987654",
    },
    "meal_preference": "vegetarian",
    "max_price_usd": 1200,
}

tools = [
    {
        "name": "search_flights",
        "description": (
            "Search for available flights between two airports for a given date and cabin class. "
            "Returns a list of flight options with prices, airlines, stops, and duration."
        ),
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "origin": {"type": "string", "description": "IATA airport code, e.g. LHR"},
                "destination": {"type": "string", "description": "IATA airport code, e.g. NRT"},
                "departure_date": {"type": "string", "description": "ISO 8601 date"},
                "return_date": {"type": "string", "description": "ISO 8601 date for return, optional"},
                "cabin_class": {"type": "string", "enum": ["economy", "premium_economy", "business", "first"]},
                "passengers": {"type": "integer", "default": 1},
                "max_price_usd": {"type": "number"},
            },
            "required": ["origin", "destination", "departure_date", "cabin_class"],
        },
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Multi-leg itinerary builder

import anthropic
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class TripRequest:
    traveller_name: str
    origin: str
    destinations: list[str]           # Ordered IATA city codes
    start_date: str
    nights_per_destination: list[int] # Nights at each stop
    budget_usd: float
    cabin_class: str
    hotel_tier: str                   # "budget" / "midscale" / "upscale" / "luxury"

itinerary_tools = [
    {
        "name": "search_flights_segment",
        "description": "Search for a single flight segment between two cities on a specific date.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "from_city": {"type": "string"},
                "to_city": {"type": "string"},
                "date": {"type": "string"},
                "cabin": {"type": "string"},
            },
            "required": ["from_city", "to_city", "date", "cabin"],
        },
    },
    {
        "name": "search_hotels",
        "description": "Search for hotels at a destination for a stay duration.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "city": {"type": "string"},
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Real-world example

Google Travel's AI-powered trip planning integrates flight and hotel search with the user's Gmail for automatic confirmation import and itinerary tracking. When a user searches for a destination, the system applies price-tracking data (Google Flights' price history) to surface whether the current fare is high, typical, or low relative to historical prices for that route and date range. This context transforms a raw price into an actionable recommendation: book now versus wait. The system also automatically imports flight confirmations from Gmail and adds them to Google Calendar with check-in reminders, handling the itinerary assembly step that booking agents would otherwise need to do programmatically.

Navan (formerly TripActions), a corporate travel management platform, implements a booking agent pattern at enterprise scale. The system encodes each company's travel policy as a structured rule set and filters search results to show only compliant options before presenting them to the traveller. Non-compliant bookings require an out-of-policy justification and manager approval, which the system routes automatically. The platform processes millions of booking events per year and reports that policy-compliant bookings account for over 90 percent of transactions, up from 60 to 70 percent under purely manual policy enforcement.
Common mistakes
Triggering payment without an explicit confirmation step

— a booking agent with autonomous payment authority will eventually book the wrong itinerary. The confirmation gate is not optional; it is the primary safeguard against irreversible financial mistakes.
Assuming prices are stable between search and booking

— flight prices are dynamic and can change in seconds. Building a flow that quotes a price and then books it minutes later without re-confirming the price at booking time will produce mismatches between the quoted and charged amount.
Hard-coding provider API schemas

— GDS and OTA APIs change their response schemas and deprecate fields without long notice periods. Build the normalisation layer as an explicit adapter for each provider; do not parse raw provider responses directly in the agent logic.
Ignoring loyalty programme application rules

— applying a frequent flyer number to a booking requires the booking to be on an eligible carrier, in an eligible fare class, and the number must be present at booking time, not added later. Getting this wrong means points are not earned, which erodes trust in the agent.
Not handling hold expiry

— a 24-hour flight hold that expires before the user confirms the booking releases the seat and may result in a price change. The agent must track hold expiry times and notify the user before the hold lapses, not after.
Over-relying on email parsing for confirmation extraction

— email templates from airlines and hotels change frequently. A brittle parser that extracts PNR numbers from a specific email format will break silently when the provider updates their template. Use structured API callbacks (webhook or API polling) where available, and treat email parsing as a fallback.
Best practices
Version the preference model

— treat the user's travel preferences as a versioned JSON document that the user can inspect, edit, and approve. Preferences encoded only in agent behaviour (never surfaced to the user) drift over time and become incorrect without the user knowing.
Fan out search queries in parallel

— sequential API calls to five different providers produce unacceptable latency. Fan out all search queries simultaneously and aggregate results as they arrive, with a timeout after which partial results are presented rather than waiting for the slowest provider.
Use a hold rather than a direct booking as the confirmation target

— proposing a 24-hour hold gives the user time to review at their pace. The hold is reversible at no cost; a booked ticket may incur a change or cancellation fee. Structuring the flow around holds rather than immediate bookings reduces the cost of user second thoughts.
Explain trade-offs in the recommendation

— present the top option with a brief explanation of the trade-offs: "This JAL flight is $130 more than the cheapest option but is non-stop, earns 4,500 loyalty points on your JAL number, and guarantees a window seat." Users are more likely to accept a slightly more expensive recommendation when the reasoning is transparent.
Monitor post-booking itineraries proactively

— the agent's value does not end at booking. Proactive monitoring for schedule changes, cancellations, and seat reassignments, with automatic rebooking proposals, is a high-value differentiator that manual travellers cannot replicate.
Apply the principle of minimal booking authority

— scope the agent's API keys to the minimum necessary permissions: search + read + hold, not full booking authority. Elevate to full booking authority only at the confirmed checkout step, and only for the specific booking being confirmed.
Performance & scalability

The primary performance challenge for a booking agent is search latency. Users expect flight and hotel results within 3 to 5 seconds of submitting a request. The bottleneck is the fan-out to multiple search APIs, each of which may take 1 to 3 seconds to respond. The solution is parallel fan-out with a progressive rendering model: present results as they arrive from each provider rather than waiting for all providers to respond, with a hard timeout after which the search is considered complete.

For the ranking function, cache the preference model in memory for the session rather than loading it from the database on every tool call. The model changes infrequently and is read-heavy; caching eliminates a database round-trip per search iteration. For enterprise deployments with multiple concurrent users, cache at the session level rather than per-request.

Itinerary monitoring at scale requires a separate asynchronous pipeline. Polling airline APIs for every booked PNR every hour is feasible for small deployments; at enterprise scale, subscribe to change notification webhooks where available (Amadeus push notifications, airline operational APIs) and use polling only as a fallback for providers without webhook support. Route change events to a queue processor that applies the rebooking logic asynchronously and delivers a notification to the user rather than blocking a foreground request.
Tooling & ecosystem
Category
Tools
Agent role
Flight search
Amadeus Flights API, Skyscanner API, Google Flights (unofficial)
Search and compare flight options; seat map queries
Hotel search
Booking.com Affiliate API, Expedia Partner Solutions, Hotels.com
Search accommodation; check availability and pricing
Car rental
Rentalcars.com API, Hertz / Avis partner APIs
Ground transport at origin or destination
Activities
Viator API, GetYourGuide Partner API
Experiences and tours at each destination
Itinerary management
TripIt API, TripCase, Google Calendar API
Store confirmed bookings; export to calendar; share itinerary
Price monitoring
Google Flights Price Calendar, Hopper (B2B), Fare Alerts APIs
Watch for price drops on target routes post-search
Corporate travel
Navan (TripActions) API, Concur Travel API, Egencia
Policy enforcement, approval workflows, expense sync
Payment
Stripe Checkout (redirect), Braintree, provider-native checkout
Payment hand-off; the agent never stores card data directly
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Booking agent (this pattern)
Handles complex constraints; preference persistence; proactive monitoring; multi-provider comparison
Engineering effort; API costs per search; confirmation gate adds a step
Manual search (user does it)
No latency, no errors, full user control over each decision
Time-intensive; same constraints re-applied manually every trip; no post-booking monitoring
Traditional OTA (Expedia, Kayak)
Single site, proven UX, handles payment natively
No preference persistence; no constraint-aware ranking; no corporate policy enforcement; no proactive monitoring
Corporate TMC (travel management company)
Human agents, complex policy enforcement, compliance reporting
High per-booking cost; slower than automated search; limited to business hours
Static booking link (Calendly-style)
Simple, no maintenance, works for simple point-to-point
No constraint matching; no loyalty application; no multi-leg support; no change management
Security & reliability

Payment card data must never pass through the agent. The correct architecture is: the agent generates a booking intent (selected flight ID, passenger details, loyalty number) and redirects the user to the provider's authenticated checkout page via a secure deep link. The provider handles PCI-compliant payment collection. The agent receives a booking confirmation back via webhook or API polling after payment completes. This boundary keeps the agent outside PCI DSS scope.

API keys for booking providers carry financial authority and must be treated as high-value credentials. Store them in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault), rotate them on a schedule, and scope each key to the minimum required permission level: read-only keys for search operations, write-enabled keys only for the booking confirmation step and only instantiated for the duration of that step. Audit logs for all booking write operations must be retained for compliance purposes.
Prompt injection via itinerary content

— adversarial content in hotel names, airline descriptions, or search result fields that the agent processes could attempt to manipulate agent behaviour. Treat all data returned from external APIs as untrusted input; sanitise before including in agent prompts.
Confirmation spoofing

— a malicious actor with access to the confirmation step could approve a booking they did not initiate. Bind confirmations to the session that generated the proposal; never accept a confirmation code from a different session or context.
Duplicate booking

— network errors during the booking API call can result in a retry that creates a second booking. Use idempotency keys on all booking API calls and check for existing bookings with the same PNR parameters before processing a retry.
Hold expiry race conditions

— if a hold expires between the user approving and the booking API call completing, the seat may be gone. The agent must handle the expired hold error gracefully: re-search, find the next best option, and surface the updated recommendation without treating the expiry as a system failure.
Key takeaways

A booking and travel agent automates constraint-aware search across multiple providers and applies a persistent preference model to rank and recommend options, but always requires user confirmation before any payment-bearing action.
The confirmation gate is non-negotiable: payment authority should never be delegated fully to the agent. Use the hold-then-confirm flow to give users time to review before money is committed.
Fan out search queries to multiple providers in parallel; sequential API calls to five providers will not meet latency expectations. Design the result presentation for progressive rendering.

Encode preferences and travel policies as structured, versioned JSON documents that users can inspect and edit directly. Preferences encoded only in agent behaviour become stale and incorrect without the user's awareness.
Payment card data must never pass through the agent. Route users to the provider's native checkout; receive confirmation back via webhook. This keeps the agent outside PCI DSS scope.
Post-booking monitoring for schedule changes and cancellations is a high-value differentiator. The agent's usefulness extends through the trip, not just at booking time.
Treat all data returned from external booking APIs as untrusted input. Sanitise before including in agent prompts to prevent prompt injection via malicious itinerary content.

For corporate deployments, model the travel policy as a structured rule set applied as a filter before results are presented, not as a post-hoc compliance check. This eliminates non-compliant options from the choice set before the user sees them.
